result.markdown:
# Importing data We introduce the most common ways to get data into Python — loading built-in datasets via seaborn, and importing CSV and Excel files — and cover how to save DataFrames back to files. {{< video https://vimeo.com/1195888052/adbbfb2ebb?fl=ml&fe=ec >}} ```python import pandas as pd import seaborn as sns # ── Loading a built-in dataset with seaborn ────────────────── titanic = sns.load_dataset("titanic") titanic.shape titanic.head() # Important checks # titanic.info() # titanic.describe() # ── Saving data to a file ──────────────────────────────────── # Save to CSV titanic.to_csv("data/titanic.csv", index=False) print("Saved to data/titanic.csv") # Save to Excel titanic.to_excel("data/titanic.xlsx", index=False, sheet_name="titanic") print("Saved to data/titanic.xlsx") # ── Loading from a file ────────────────────────────────────── titanic_from_csv = pd.read_csv("data/titanic.csv") titanic_from_csv.head() titanic_from_excel = pd.read_excel("data/titanic.xlsx", sheet_name="titanic") titanic_from_excel.head() # ── The sep parameter for European CSV files ───────────────── # Save a semicolon-separated version to demonstrate titanic.to_csv("data/titanic_semicolon.csv", index=False, sep=";") # Load it back — must specify sep=";" titanic_semi = pd.read_csv("data/titanic_semicolon.csv", sep =";") titanic_semi.head() # ── Saving and loading the other datasets ──────────────────── healthexp = sns.load_dataset("healthexp") flights = sns.load_dataset("flights") healthexp.to_csv("data/healthexp.csv", index=False) flights.to_csv("data/flights.csv", index=False) print("healthexp saved:", healthexp.shape) print("flights saved:", flights.shape) ``` ## Exercises ### Exercise 3.1.1 The code from this video saves the Titanic dataset to your `data/` folder. Before moving on, make sure you have run that code and that the file exists on your computer. Open the file browser in Positron (the folder icon in the left sidebar) and navigate to your `data/` folder. Confirm that `titanic.csv` is there. Then load the file and use `.info()` and `.describe()` to answer the following questions: 1. How many passengers are in the dataset? 2. Which columns have missing values, and how many values are missing in each? 3. What is the mean fare paid by passengers?
Solution ::: {#e956c847 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code} import pandas as pd titanic = pd.read_csv("data/titanic.csv") titanic.info() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` RangeIndex: 891 entries, 0 to 890 Data columns (total 15 columns): # Column Non-Null Count Dtype --- ------ -------------- ----- 0 survived 891 non-null int64 1 pclass 891 non-null int64 2 sex 891 non-null str 3 age 714 non-null float64 4 sibsp 891 non-null int64 5 parch 891 non-null int64 6 fare 891 non-null float64 7 embarked 889 non-null str 8 class 891 non-null str 9 who 891 non-null str 10 adult_male 891 non-null bool 11 deck 203 non-null str 12 embark_town 889 non-null str 13 alive 891 non-null str 14 alone 891 non-null bool dtypes: bool(2), float64(2), int64(4), str(7) memory usage: 92.4 KB ``` ::: ::: ::: {#e301bfc2 .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} titanic.describe() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=2} ```{=html}
survived pclass age sibsp parch fare
count 891.000000 891.000000 714.000000 891.000000 891.000000 891.000000
mean 0.383838 2.308642 29.699118 0.523008 0.381594 32.204208
std 0.486592 0.836071 14.526497 1.102743 0.806057 49.693429
min 0.000000 1.000000 0.420000 0.000000 0.000000 0.000000
25% 0.000000 2.000000 20.125000 0.000000 0.000000 7.910400
50% 0.000000 3.000000 28.000000 0.000000 0.000000 14.454200
75% 1.000000 3.000000 38.000000 1.000000 0.000000 31.000000
max 1.000000 3.000000 80.000000 8.000000 6.000000 512.329200
``` ::: ::: 1. There are 891 passengers (rows) in the dataset. 2. Four columns have missing values: `age` (177 missing), `embarked` (2 missing), `deck` (688 missing) and `embark_town` (2 missing). You can read this from the `Non-Null Count` column in the `.info()` output — a column with 891 non-null values has no missing values, while fewer means some are missing. 3. The mean fare is approximately 32.20, visible in the `mean` row of the `.describe()` output.
--- ### Exercise 3.1.2 The video code at the bottom of the script saves the `healthexp` and `flights` datasets to your `data/` folder. Run that code now if you have not already done so, then complete the steps below. **Step 1.** Open the file browser in Positron and navigate to your `data/` folder. Confirm that `healthexp.csv` and `flights.csv` are both there. **Step 2.** Find the full absolute path to your `data/` folder. You can do this by right-clicking the `data/` folder in the Positron file browser and selecting "Copy Path", or by running the following code in the console: ```python import os print(os.path.abspath("data")) ``` Write down or copy this path — you will need it in the next exercise. **Step 3.** Load both files using `pd.read_csv()` and print the shape of each.
Solution ::: {#65081484 .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} import pandas as pd healthexp = pd.read_csv("data/healthexp.csv") flights = pd.read_csv("data/flights.csv") print(healthexp.shape) print(flights.shape) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` (274, 4) (144, 3) ``` ::: ::: `healthexp` has 274 rows and 4 columns. `flights` has 144 rows and 3 columns. The absolute path printed by `os.path.abspath("data")` will look different on every computer, for example: - Windows: `C:\Users\yourname\Documents\skl401\data` - Mac: `/Users/yourname/Documents/skl401/data` This is the full address of the folder from the root of your file system. The relative path `"data/titanic.csv"` is a shortcut that only works because Python is currently running from your project folder — the folder that contains the `data/` subfolder.
--- ### Exercise 3.1.3 This exercise explores the difference between relative and absolute paths, and what can go wrong when you use the wrong one. Run the following code and look at the path that is printed: ```python import os print(os.getcwd()) ``` `os.getcwd()` returns the **working directory** — the folder Python is currently treating as its starting point. When you use a relative path like `"data/titanic.csv"`, Python looks for a `data/` folder inside this location. Now answer the following questions: 1. Does the path printed match the project folder you set up at the start of the course — the folder that contains your scripts and your `data/` subfolder? 2. Load `titanic.csv` using the full absolute path you found in Exercise 3.1.2 instead of the relative path. Confirm the result is identical. Note: if you are on Windows, your path will use backslashes (e.g. `C:\Users\yourname\...`). Python requires forward slashes in paths, so replace any backslashes with forward slashes before running the code. 3. Suppose a classmate copies only the script file to their Desktop and runs it from there, without copying the `data/` folder. Which path would work for them — the relative path or the absolute path you used in step 2? Explain why.
Solution ```python import os print(os.getcwd()) ``` ```python # Replace this path with the absolute path on your own computer titanic_abs = pd.read_csv("/Users/yourname/Documents/skl401/data/titanic.csv") titanic_abs.shape ``` 1. Yes — if you opened Positron from your project folder and are running scripts from there, `os.getcwd()` should return that folder. If it shows something unexpected, check that Positron has the right folder open (Open → Open Folder...). 2. The result is identical — `read_csv()` does not care whether you provide a relative or absolute path, as long as the path points to the same file. 3. Neither path would work as-is. The relative path `"data/titanic.csv"` would fail because there is no `data/` folder on the Desktop. The absolute path would also fail because it points to a location on *your* computer, not theirs. To share a script that others can run, you either need to share the whole project folder (scripts and data together), or load data from a URL. Hard-coding absolute paths into scripts is a common mistake — they silently break the moment anyone else tries to run the code.
--- ### Exercise 3.1.4 The code below saves a small DataFrame to CSV and loads it back in. Run it and inspect the result. ```python import pandas as pd products = pd.DataFrame({ "product": ["coffee", "tea", "juice"], "price": [29.90, 35.00, 12.50], "quantity": [3, 5, 0] }) products.to_csv("data/products.csv") products_loaded = pd.read_csv("data/products.csv") print(products_loaded) ``` 1. How many columns does `products_loaded` have compared to `products`? What is the extra column called, and where did it come from? 2. Fix the `to_csv()` call so that the extra column does not appear when you reload the file.
Solution 1. `products_loaded` has 4 columns instead of 3. The extra column is called `Unnamed: 0` and contains the numbers 0, 1, 2. It comes from the row index — when you save a DataFrame without specifying `index=False`, pandas writes the index as the first column in the file. When you reload the file, pandas reads that column as ordinary data because it has no way of knowing it was the index. ::: {#28985849 .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} import pandas as pd products = pd.DataFrame({ "product": ["coffee", "tea", "juice"], "price": [29.90, 35.00, 12.50], "quantity": [3, 5, 0] }) products.to_csv("data/products.csv") products_loaded = pd.read_csv("data/products.csv") print(products_loaded) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Unnamed: 0 product price quantity 0 0 coffee 29.9 3 1 1 tea 35.0 5 2 2 juice 12.5 0 ``` ::: ::: 2. Add `index=False` to the `to_csv()` call: ::: {#a0652fe2 .cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code} import pandas as pd products = pd.DataFrame({ "product": ["coffee", "tea", "juice"], "price": [29.90, 35.00, 12.50], "quantity": [3, 5, 0] }) products.to_csv("data/products.csv", index=False) products_loaded = pd.read_csv("data/products.csv") print(products_loaded) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` product price quantity 0 coffee 29.9 3 1 tea 35.0 5 2 juice 12.5 0 ``` ::: ::: This is one of the most common mistakes when saving DataFrames. Always use `index=False` unless you have specifically set a meaningful index (such as a date or a country name) that you want to preserve.
--- ### Exercise 3.1.5 A colleague sends you a CSV file exported from Excel. When you try to load it, something looks wrong: ```python import pandas as pd df = pd.read_csv("data/titanic_semicolon.csv") print(df.shape) print(df.head()) ``` Instead of 891 rows and 15 columns, the output shows 891 rows and **1 column**, and the single column contains values like `"survived;pclass;sex;age;fare;..."`. 1. What has gone wrong? 2. Fix the `read_csv()` call so the data loads correctly with all 15 columns. Note: the file `titanic_semicolon.csv` was created by the video code. If you do not have it yet, run this first: ```python titanic = pd.read_csv("data/titanic.csv") titanic.to_csv("data/titanic_semicolon.csv", index=False, sep=";") ```
Solution 1. The file uses semicolons as the separator between columns instead of commas. By default, `read_csv()` expects commas. When it finds none, it treats each entire row as a single value and returns a DataFrame with one column containing all the fields joined together. 2. Add `sep=";"` to tell pandas to split on semicolons instead: ::: {#b2fb4ac4 .cell execution_count=6} ``` {.python .cell-code} import pandas as pd df = pd.read_csv("data/titanic_semicolon.csv", sep=";") print(df.shape) print(df.head()) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` (891, 15) survived pclass sex age sibsp parch fare embarked class \ 0 0 3 male 22.0 1 0 7.2500 S Third 1 1 1 female 38.0 1 0 71.2833 C First 2 1 3 female 26.0 0 0 7.9250 S Third 3 1 1 female 35.0 1 0 53.1000 S First 4 0 3 male 35.0 0 0 8.0500 S Third who adult_male deck embark_town alive alone 0 man True NaN Southampton no False 1 woman False C Cherbourg yes False 2 woman False NaN Southampton yes True 3 woman False C Southampton yes False 4 man True NaN Southampton no True ``` ::: ::: Semicolon-separated files are common in Europe, where the comma is used as the decimal separator in numbers. If you ever load a CSV and get a single column with all the data crammed together, a mismatched separator is almost always the cause. Open the raw file in a text editor to see which separator it actually uses.
